method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is translated as “western island”.[2]  
The island was first recorded by the Spanish ship Rosario in 1702 and was known as Rosario Island before it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3]  
English-language sources also sometimes render the name with a hyphen as Nishino-shima.[5]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island on the volcanic front of the Izu–Bonin arc, about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Islands.[4] The volcano lies on the north–south-trending Shichitō–Iōjima Ridge, parallel to the Ogasawara Trench, where volcanism is driven by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The island forms the emergent summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the island-forming main peak, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]

Nishinoshima has generally been described as an andesitic volcanic island, with erupted materials from earlier activity containing 51.8–60.1 wt% SiO₂.[1] Studies of the 2020 submarine deposits showed a broader range of magma compositions, including basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite.[3] The andesites erupted since 1973 are thought to derive from primary magmas generated directly in the mantle by shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] Basalts erupted in 2020 resemble older basalts from surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma may have been produced throughout the volcano’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the pre-existing island.[1] This eruption started as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion, shifted semicircularly, and produced five craters that emitted volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] A new island was observed in September 1973, and later deposits connected the new land to the older island.[1] By 1982, sediment movement and volcanic deposits had closed the mouth of the bay between the old and new landforms.[1]

A major eruptive sequence from 2013 to 2020 has been divided into four episodes, with the first lasting about two years, the second about 120 days, the third about a week, and the fourth about 270 days.[3] Estimated average lava effusion rates were about 2.0 × 10⁵ m³ per day during the 2013–2015 episode and about 1.5 × 10⁵ m³ per day during the 2017 episode.[3] During the fourth episode in 2019–2020, the eruption style changed from Strombolian activity with lava effusion to lava fountains and then to ash emission probably generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[4] This transition to sustained fine-ash-producing explosive activity had not been observed in previous recorded eruptions at Nishinoshima.[4]

Because of Nishinoshima’s remote location, recent eruptive activity has been monitored mainly by satellites, aircraft, and research vessels.[4] Optical satellite imagery and synthetic-aperture radar data from missions such as TerraSAR-X and ALOS-2 have been used to track changes in the eruption and island growth over time.[4] Synthetic-aperture radar is particularly useful at Nishinoshima because its long-wavelength electromagnetic observations are not blocked by cloud cover or volcanic plumes.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima’s ecology is characterized by repeated disturbance, with eruptions and marine erosion continually altering available habitat and often removing much of the island’s terrestrial vegetation.[2][5] Because the island lies about 130 km west of Chichijima, colonization depends on long-distance dispersal from other islands and surrounding regions, making it a natural site for studying primary succession on oceanic islands.[3][4] Botanical work has specifically examined the invasion of plants onto newly formed volcanic land at Nishinoshima, reflecting its value as a field example of early ecosystem development.[4]

The island’s biological setting is part of the wider Ogasawara archipelago, whose isolated oceanic islands support distinctive ecosystems shaped by adaptive radiation, speciation, and high levels of endemism.[3][5] Compared with older islands in the chain, Nishinoshima represents an especially dynamic case in which ecological communities may be repeatedly reset before mature terrestrial ecosystems can develop.[2][3] A population of common purslane, *Portulaca oleracea*, was collected by researchers before a later eruption, but the plant was subsequently reported to have disappeared from Nishinoshima, illustrating the vulnerability of early colonists to renewed volcanic activity.[2] Research and monitoring also extend to the surrounding marine environment, including observations of coral reef development and the establishment of marine biodiversity around the island.[3]
